label: fiction
title: The Silver Hoof
charge: Killing a racehorse to defraud its insurers
elements: The accused killed it or had it done.
He acted to obtain the insurance money.

beats.text: A stable lad remembers a vagrant at the yard gate at dusk — a stranger to the district, gone by morning.
beats.reveal_note: A casual labourer walking to the next town, later found and fully alibied. The stranger at the gate is the story every inside job needs.

beats.text: The policy file: cover on the horse was trebled five weeks before its death, the new premium paid in cash by the trainer himself.
beats.reveal_note: Prudence insures a horse; design trebles it just in time — and cash keeps the increase off the stable's books.

beats.text: The night watchman: the yard dog challenges every stranger who so much as touches the gate. That night it never barked.
beats.reveal_note: Small and telling: whoever crossed the yard was known to the dog.

beats.text: The veterinary necropsy: an injection site in the neck where no treatment was recorded — and the trainer's own kit found one syringe short at inventory.
beats.reveal_note: The mechanism, physically present: a needle mark with no medical excuse, from a kit only one man kept.

beats.text: The judge: insurance is what prudent men buy. The jury must find the act, not merely the incentive.
beats.reveal_note: Correct — and satisfied: the syringe and the silent dog supply the act that the policy only motivates.

twist: It looked exactly like what it was. The trebled policy paid in cash, the dog that knew its visitor, and the missing syringe: the intruder theory needed a stranger, and the yard had none that night.
